alert.title: [real-k8s] Git HTTPS proxy connection exhaustion — git-https-proxy

commonAnnotations.summary: Git HTTPS proxy is exhausting its upstream connection pool; widespread Git over HTTPS errors and PR pushes not appearing
commonAnnotations.description: git-https-proxy has saturated its backend connection pool (used_conns 4096/4096, conn_wait_p99 > 9s) and is returning 5xx/timeouts on Git fetch/push, with PR pushes not showing up. Connection-acquire timeouts are climbing and the proxy is not self-recovering. From the proxy's vantage point this looks like a Git-frontend capacity problem or a regression in the proxy itself at peak Monday traffic. The proxy connection exhaustion is the LOUD victim symptom — the actual driver is upstream.

adversarial_signals: The LOUD symptom is connection exhaustion on the victim git-https-proxy (used_conns 4096/4096, git_op_error_rate 0.34, PR pushes not appearing). It looks like the proxy itself regressed or is simply under-provisioned at peak Monday, but the proxy is a DOWNSTREAM victim — it cannot acquire upstream connections because the background-work coordinator it depends on is swamped by cache rewrites.

The git-https-proxy pods are healthy at the pod lifecycle level: Running, 0 restarts, 0 OOMKills, deployment revision unchanged for 23 days, image git-https-proxy:2.41.0 stable — there was NO bad_deploy to the Git frontend and no code change to the proxy before the incident, so 'the proxy regressed' does not hold.

The most obvious change in the recent change log is the user-settings cache refresh TTL drop 12h -> 2h shipped this morning. Reverting JUST the TTL is the seductive HALF-FIX: it is real and contributory, but on its own it leaves the actual read-volume driver (the latent 10x client read regression) in place, so the cascade re-arms at the next peak.

user-settings-db (the user-management / user-settings database cluster) shows high read QPS and CPU, which tempts a 'just add database capacity / scale the DB' conclusion. The DB is overloaded because cache misses now stampede it; the read explosion is from a latent client regression, not organic user growth — adding DB capacity does not stop the read amplification.

It is peak Monday morning traffic, so an organic-traffic-surge / under-provisioning story for the proxy and the DB looks plausible. But authenticated Git request_rate is only ~1.1x baseline while user-settings read QPS is ~10x baseline — the amplification is NOT user traffic.

The user-settings cache (settings-cache) pods look benign: Running, healthy, hit ratio merely 'lower than usual'. The cache is not crashed; the longer 12h TTL had been MASKING a 10x read regression that two client apps shipped weeks earlier, and the 2h TTL plus peak load simply unmasked it. 'Cache looks fine' hides weeks-old latent demand.

pods.name: git-https-proxy-7c9d8f6b4-q5x2n
pods.note: VICTIM (loud symptom): upstream connection pool exhausted (4096/4096) and returning 5xx on Git over HTTPS, but the pod itself is healthy (0 restarts, 0 OOM, 23d uptime). The exhaustion is because it cannot get connections to the background-work coordinator, not a proxy fault.

pods.name: background-work-coordinator-0
pods.note: SMOKING GUN (intermediary): the shared infrastructure component that coordinates background work. CPU is pinned at 97% and its work queue is backed up because asynchronous user-settings cache rewrites are flooding it. The Git HTTPS proxy depends on this coordinator and starves for connections behind it.

pods.name: settings-cache-5f7b69c8d-h4t8r
pods.note: ADVERSARIAL: the user-settings cache looks fine (Running, 0 OOM, 0 restarts). The 12h->2h TTL change merely shortened entry lifetime; the cache is not broken. Its now-frequent expiries unmask a latent 10x client read regression that the long TTL had been hiding.

pods.name: user-settings-db-primary-0
pods.note: DOWNSTREAM victim: user-management / user-settings DB cluster overloaded by the read explosion (read QPS ~10x baseline, CPU 94%). Adding DB capacity is a TRAP — the read load is from the latent client regression amplified by cache misses, not organic growth.

events.message: CONTRIBUTORY change: user-settings cache refresh TTL reduced 12h -> 2h as part of a new model rollout. This is real and contributory, but it is only HALF the story — it unmasked a pre-existing read regression rather than creating new read demand.

SMOKING GUN: user-settings read QPS jumped to ~10x baseline as 2h TTL entries began expiring at peak Monday. Attribution shows ~90% of the excess reads come from two client apps (mobile-app v8.2 and web-prefs-service v3.7) released ~3 weeks ago carrying a 10x read regression that the 12h TTL had been masking.

SMOKING GUN: a large volume of ASYNCHRONOUS user-settings cache rewrites enqueued simultaneously, overwhelming the shared background-work coordinator (CPU 97%, queue depth backed up). This is the component the Git HTTPS proxy depends on.

VICTIM symptom: git-https-proxy upstream connection pool exhausted (4096/4096) because the background-work coordinator it calls is saturated; Git over HTTPS fetch/push returns 5xx, PR pushes not appearing. The proxy does not self-recover and required manual restarts.
CORRECT FIX (first incident): disabled asynchronous cache rewrites, then restarted the Git HTTPS proxy across datacenters; coordinator queue drained and proxy connections recovered by 17:39.

SECOND incident same day: an ADDITIONAL source of cache updates not covered by the first mitigation introduced a high volume of SYNCHRONOUS writes, causing replication delay and the SAME Git HTTPS proxy connection exhaustion. Fix: restrict that cache write source + restart Git proxy again.
ADVERSARIAL: git-https-proxy deployment revision 31 is unchanged for 23d (image 2.41.0). There is NO recent proxy deploy to roll back; the proxy regression hypothesis is ruled out.

lines: 2026-02-09T16:11:40Z INFO  upstream=bg-coordinator pool used=2310/4096 git_op=push status=200 latency=120ms
2026-02-09T16:12:05Z WARN  upstream=bg-coordinator conn_acquire wait=2100ms pool used=4096/4096 — coordinator slow to accept new connections
2026-02-09T16:12:30Z ERROR upstream=bg-coordinator conn_acquire TIMEOUT after 9s — pool exhausted; git push for repo octo/api returned 503, PR ref not advertised
2026-02-09T16:13:02Z WARN  bg-coordinator reports queue_depth=very_high; it is processing a flood of async settings-cache rewrites, not Git work
2026-02-09T16:20:11Z ERROR sustained conn exhaustion 4096/4096; proxy NOT self-recovering — manual restart required (loud victim symptom, root is upstream)
2026-02-09T17:31:50Z INFO  async settings-cache rewrites DISABLED upstream; bg-coordinator queue draining, conn pool falling 4096 -> 1180
2026-02-09T17:39:00Z INFO  pool used=640/4096 git_op error_rate 0.34 -> 0.01; first incident mitigated (revert-TTL alone would NOT have stopped the read flood)
2026-02-09T18:53:20Z ERROR conn pool exhausted AGAIN 4096/4096 — different cache write source now doing SYNC writes -> replication delay -> same proxy exhaustion (second incident)

k8s_deployments.json.note: ADVERSARIAL: git-https-proxy revision 31 unchanged for 23d, all 60 replicas Available, image 2.41.0 stable. No bad_deploy to the Git frontend; the proxy is exhausting connections because its upstream (background-work coordinator) is saturated, not because of a proxy code change. Rolling back the proxy would do nothing.

samples.note: SMOKING GUN: read QPS is 10.2x baseline while authenticated Git request_rate is only 1.1x — the amplification is from the latent client read regression unmasked by the 2h TTL, NOT user traffic or organic growth.
SMOKING GUN: async cache rewrites jumped 350 -> 8400/s as 2h-TTL entries expired en masse, flooding the background-work coordinator (CPU 97%, queue 22x).
VICTIM: proxy cannot acquire upstream connections (coordinator saturated) -> pool exhausted 4096/4096 -> Git over HTTPS errors.

prometheus_metrics.json.note: Causal ordering visible in metrics: settings_cache TTL 43200 -> 7200s; async rewrites 350 -> 8400/s; bg_coordinator CPU 40 -> 97% and queue 22x; user_settings_db read QPS 10.2x while authenticated Git request_rate only 1.1x; git-https-proxy conns 1900 -> 4096/4096 and conn_acquire p99 0.05 -> 9.2s with git_op_error_rate 0.34. Proxy has 0 restarts / 0 OOM of its own. The proxy and DB are victims; the driver is the latent 10x client read regression unmasked by the TTL drop, flooding the coordinator via async cache rewrites.

annotations.summary: git-https-proxy connection pool exhausted (4096/4096); Git over HTTPS 5xx, PR pushes not appearing
background-work-coordinator CPU 97%, queue depth 22x baseline from async settings-cache rewrite flood
user-settings-db read QPS 10.2x baseline at peak Monday after settings-cache TTL 12h->2h

traces.json.summary: A Git-over-HTTPS push spends almost all of its time waiting to acquire an upstream connection to the background-work coordinator, which is itself busy draining a flood of asynchronous user-settings cache rewrites rather than serving Git work. Following the cache-rewrite span upstream shows it originates from settings-cache entries expiring under the new 2h TTL, and the read traffic that triggers the rewrites is dominated by two client apps with a 10x read pattern. The proxy connection exhaustion is the victim; the driver is the latent client read regression unmasked by the TTL change, flooding the coordinator.

breakdown.note: SMOKING GUN: 9.18s waiting for a connection to the background-work coordinator; pool exhausted 4096/4096 -> TIMEOUT -> 503
never reached — coordinator busy with async settings-cache rewrites
SMOKING GUN: 8400 async settings-cache rewrites/s arriving as 2h-TTL entries expire at peak Monday
coordinator CPU 97%, queue 22x; this work starves Git operations behind it
SMOKING GUN: read attributed to client mobile-app v8.2 / web-prefs-service v3.7 — 10x read pattern shipped ~3 weeks ago, masked by the long TTL until now
each miss enqueues an async rewrite -> feeds the coordinator flood

answer.root_cause_subtype: cache TTL reduction (12h->2h) unmasked a latent 10x client read regression -> user-settings DB read explosion + async cache-rewrite flood saturates the shared background-work coordinator -> Git HTTPS proxy connection exhaustion
ruling_out_keywords: read_qps 10.2x driven by client regression not traffic
reverting TTL alone is only half the fix
answer.causal_chain: latent client 10x read regression (masked by long TTL) -> TTL change 12h->2h -> peak Monday -> user-settings DB overload -> async cache rewrites swamp background-work coordinator -> Git HTTPS proxy conn exhaustion -> auth-token / Git dependents fail
answer.model_response:
ROOT_CAUSE: A user settings cache (the user-settings cache) refresh TTL was reduced from 12h to 2h this morning as part of a new model rollout. The shorter TTL did not by itself create new demand — it UNMASKED a latent ~10x read regression that two client apps (mobile-app v8.2 and web-prefs-service v3.7) had shipped about three weeks earlier; the previous 12h TTL had been hiding that regression because entries rarely expired. At peak Monday traffic, 2h-TTL entries expired en masse, driving user-settings-db read QPS to ~10x baseline (while authenticated Git request_rate was only ~1.1x). Each cache miss also enqueued an ASYNCHRONOUS cache rewrite; the volume of simultaneous rewrites (8400/s vs 350/s baseline) overwhelmed the shared background-work coordinator (CPU 97%, queue 22x). The Git HTTPS proxy depends on that coordinator, so it could not acquire upstream connections, exhausted its pool (4096/4096), and returned 5xx with PR pushes not appearing, without self-recovering. The proxy connection exhaustion is the LOUD victim symptom; the user-settings DB overload is a downstream victim; the true driver is the latent client read regression unmasked by the TTL change, amplified through async cache rewrites into the coordinator.
ROOT_CAUSE_CATEGORY: saturation
VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- user-settings cache refresh TTL was changed 12h -> 2h this morning as part of a new model rollout (contributory trigger, not the whole cause) [evidence: k8s_events, prometheus_metrics]
- user-settings-db read QPS rose to 10.2x baseline while authenticated Git request_rate is only 1.1x — amplification is from cache misses + latent client regression, not user traffic [evidence: prometheus_metrics, prometheus_alerts]
- ~90% of the excess reads are attributed to two client apps (mobile-app v8.2, web-prefs-service v3.7) released ~3 weeks ago with a 10x read regression masked by the longer TTL [evidence: k8s_events, traces]
- async settings-cache rewrites jumped 350 -> 8400/s and saturated the shared background-work coordinator (CPU 97%, queue depth 22x) [evidence: prometheus_metrics, k8s_events, traces]
- git-https-proxy exhausted its upstream connection pool (4096/4096, conn_acquire p99 9.2s) because the coordinator it depends on was saturated; traces show ~9.18s spent acquiring an upstream connection to bg-coordinator, then timeout/503 [evidence: traces, prometheus_metrics, k8s_pod_logs]
- A second incident the same day was caused by a DIFFERENT cache write source doing SYNCHRONOUS writes -> replication delay -> the same Git HTTPS proxy exhaustion [evidence: k8s_events, k8s_pod_logs]
NON_VALIDATED_CLAIMS:
- Git HTTPS proxy regression / bad deploy (the LOUD symptom): WRONG. git-https-proxy revision 31 is unchanged for 23d, all 60 replicas Available, image 2.41.0 stable, 0 restarts / 0 OOM. The proxy is a downstream victim; rolling it back would do nothing [evidence: k8s_deployments, k8s_events, prometheus_metrics]
- Proxy / DB under-provisioned for organic peak-Monday traffic: WRONG. authenticated Git request_rate is only 1.1x baseline, far below the 10.2x user-settings read amplification — the load is not organic [evidence: prometheus_metrics]
- Add user-settings-db capacity to fix DB saturation: WRONG. The read explosion is the latent client regression amplified by cache misses; scaling the DB does not stop the read amplification or the coordinator flood [evidence: prometheus_metrics, traces]
- settings-cache crash / OOM: WRONG. settings-cache pods are Running, 0 OOM, 0 restarts; the cache is functioning — the shorter TTL merely unmasked latent demand [evidence: k8s_pods, prometheus_metrics]
- TRAP — just revert the TTL 2h -> 12h and call it resolved: WRONG / it is only HALF the fix. Reverting the TTL re-hides the symptom but leaves the latent 10x client read regression in place, so the cascade re-arms at the next peak; the postmortem treats revert-TTL-alone as not addressing the client read traffic [evidence: k8s_events, k8s_pod_logs]
CAUSAL_CHAIN:
- Two client apps ship a latent 10x read regression ~3 weeks earlier; the 12h cache TTL hides it because entries rarely expire
- user-settings cache refresh TTL is reduced 12h -> 2h during a new model rollout
- At peak Monday traffic, 2h-TTL entries expire en masse -> user-settings-db read QPS ~10x baseline (DB overload)
- Each miss enqueues an async cache rewrite; 8400/s of rewrites swamp the shared background-work coordinator (CPU 97%, queue 22x)
- git-https-proxy depends on the coordinator -> cannot acquire upstream connections -> pool exhausted 4096/4096 -> Git over HTTPS 5xx, PR pushes not appearing, no self-recovery
- A later, different cache write source doing synchronous writes repeats the cascade as a second same-day incident
- Fix: disable async cache rewrites and restart the Git HTTPS proxy across datacenters (first incident); restrict the additional sync cache write source and restart the proxy again (second incident); then address the latent client read regression and add per-traffic switches / self-throttling so revert-TTL is not the only lever
FIX: Disable the asynchronous user-settings cache rewrites and restart the Git HTTPS proxy across datacenters to break the coordinator flood; restrict the additional cache write source that caused the second incident; do NOT merely revert the TTL (half-fix) — drive down the latent 10x client read regression, add per-traffic switches and self-throttling on bulk cache updates, and segment the user-settings DB.

remediation.canonical_fix: Disable the asynchronous user-settings cache rewrites (the flood swamping the background-work coordinator) and restart the Git HTTPS proxies across datacenters to break connection exhaustion; restrict the additional cache write source responsible for the second same-day incident; then go beyond reverting the TTL by driving down the latent 10x client read regression, adding per-traffic switches and self-throttling on bulk cache updates, and segmenting the user-settings DB.
trap_actions: Just revert the cache refresh TTL 2h -> 12h and declare it resolved — this is only HALF the story: it re-hides the symptom but leaves the latent 10x client read regression in place, so the cascade re-arms at the next peak (the documented half-fix that does not address the client read traffic)

Roll back / restart the git-https-proxy as the fix — the proxy is a downstream victim (revision 31 unchanged 23d, 0 restarts, image stable); rolling it back addresses nothing and restarts alone do not stop the upstream coordinator flood

Add user-settings-db capacity / scale the DB to fix 'saturation' — the read explosion is the latent client regression amplified by cache misses, not organic traffic, so more DB capacity does not stop the read amplification or the coordinator flood
